parties: Between Gary G. Fitzgerald, Petitioner, and Arthur Trono, Warden of William Head Institution, Robert Lusk, Warden of Kent Institution and Deputy Commissioner of the Correctional Service of Canada, Pacific Region, Respondents
citation: [1994] B.C.J. No. 1534 DRS 94-13714 Vancouver Registry: CC931084
court: British Columbia Supreme Court Vancouver, British Columbia Thackray J.
body_html:
Prisons -- Administration -- Powers re prisoners -- Transfers -- Grounds -- Whether decision to transfer was patently unreasonable.

Petition for an order transferring a prison inmate from one institution to a higher security institution. The petitioner was serving a life sentence with no eligibility for parole for 25 years. Based on information received from another inmate that the petitioner was planning an escape, the defendants decided to transfer him to a maximum security prison. The petitioner was unable to show that the informant lied, because the respondent refused to disclose the identity of the informant. The petitioner's case management team had earlier recommended his transfer to a minimum security prison on the ground that substantial progress had been made in the rehabilitation of the petitioner. The alleged escape plot was so inept as to seem unbelievable. The issue for determination was whether the decision of the respondent met the tests of procedural fairness.

HELD: Petition allowed. The court was unable to conclude that the respondent's reasons properly reflected the evidence, including such information as was concealed from the petitioner. The decision to transfer appeared to have been extremely arbitrary and, as such, unfair. It had not been demonstrated that the decision was arrived at fairly and, accordingly, the decision was patently unreasonable.

Statutes, Regulations and Rules Cited:

Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, 1982, s. 7. Corrections and Conditional Release Act, s. 70.

Counsel for the Petitioner: John W. Conroy.

Counsel for the Respondents: David Fitzsimmons.

THACKRAY J.:— The petitioner seeks an order transferring him from Kent Institution to William Head Institution. Mr. Fitzgerald was convicted in 1981 of first degree murder which occurred during a robbery. A gas station attendant and a police officer were shot and killed by Mr. Fitzgerald. He was sentenced to life imprisonment with no eligibility for parole for 25 years. In 1984 he was found guilty by an Internal Disciplinary Court of attempted escape from Kingston Penitentiary.

PETITION

The petitioner applies to this court for an order for relief in the nature of habeas corpus pursuant to sections 774 and 482(3)(c) of the Criminal Code of Canada, the Criminal Rules, 1991 and Rules 10 and 63 of the Rules of Court and sections 10(c) and 24(1) of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, directed to the Warden of Kent Institution, ordering that the petitioner be released from Kent Institution and transferred back to William Head Institution relying on the relevant sections of the Penitentiary Act, the Penitentiary Service Regulations, the Corrections and Conditional Release Act and the Corrections and Conditional Release Act Regulations as may apply.

HISTORY OF PETITIONS

The first petition was filed November 30, 1992. It alleged breaches of the Criminal Code, Penitentiary Act and Charter. However, its thrust was that insufficient information had been provided to the petitioner. That petition was heard by Gow J. on January 6, 1993. Reasons were delivered on January 15, 1993, Victoria Registry No. 92 4007.

Those reasons reproduce portions of the Directive of the Commissioner of the Correctional Service of Canada with respect to the transfer of inmates. As well, they reproduce the Notification of Recommendation for Involuntary Transfer to a High Maximum Security Facility, the reasons of the Warden, the confirming reasons of the Assistant Deputy Commissioner, portions of affidavits from the petitioner and the Warden and a list of the authorities referred to by Gow J.

Mr. Justice Gow concluded as follows:

It clearly appears from the Warden's affidavit that what compelled him to take the steps he did was the "plan to escape" as told him by the individual who had heard it first hand. The issue before this court is - did the Warden's notification to the petitioner provide to the petitioner to the greatest extent possible the substance and details of that plan as told by that individual to the Warden?

For the court to decide that issue it must be fully informed as to what that individual told the Warden. The laconic paragraph 5 of the Warden's affidavit does not provide that full information. Nor can that full information be gathered from the language of the notification itself. That language can be construed as meaning no more than that the individual told the Warden "Fitzgerald has told me that he plans to obtain a set of false identification and then to escape by boat and travel to Spain" or that he told him, as the notification suggests, much more by way of details of and about the plan.

If this matter is to be dealt with properly, then the Warden must file by 12:00 Noon, Monday, January 25, 1993 a supplementary affidavit containing the full information required, namely, where, when and how, who told him what. If the individual's identity requires to be protected, that should be stated and why. "The mere fact that the information was provided in confidence is not in itself, sufficient reason to justify non-disclosure of that information", re Cadieux and Director of Mountain Institution (1984), 13 C.C.C. (3d), 330 at p. 346. If immunity from disclosure is sought, then I commend to counsel for the Warden the procedure of the "sealed envelope together with the specific explanation" referred to in Cadieux, supra, at p. 349.

If no affidavit is filed within the time stated, then I shall assume that the Warden does not desire to provide further information. In that event, I shall allow the application, quash the transfer decision and order that the petitioner be transferred back to the William Head Institution. If the affidavit is filed, I shall consider it and, if need be, or counsel so desire, hear further submissions before I decide the issue.

On January 20, 1993 Mr. A.M. Trono, Warden of William Head Institution, swore an affidavit. This was followed by affidavits in support of the petitioner. Mr. Justice Gow was provided with the internal investigation report into the alleged escape plan. He sealed that report. On February 5 Gow J. held a further hearing and issued reasons on February 25, 1993 in which he said:

The primary issue is whether the Warden's notification handed to the petitioner on October 19, provided to him sufficient information about the allegation that he had been planning to escape from William Head as to enable him to properly reply thereto, Bruce v. Canada (1992) 13 C.R. (4th) 384 (N.B.C.A.) or, to resort to the language of the Commissioner's directive, was there such a "sharing" with the petitioner of the information the Warden had that the details of the incident which prompted the transfer were provided to the greatest extent possible.

Gow J. concluded:

In the context of the authorities referred to in my earlier reasons and the prodigious sweep of the language of the Commissioner's directive, I am compelled to conclude that the information provided on October 19, 1992 was insufficient. The Warden should have provided then what is now provided in his supplementary affidavit.

Gow J. also made an important finding as to the "sharing" of information:

... now that by the supplementary affidavit there has been a sharing to the greatest extent possible of the details of the alleged plan to escape, the petitioner should be given the opportunity to make a response as provided for by paragraph 15 of the Commissioner's directive, and should he do so, the Warden must consider it.

The reasons then provide that upon receipt by the Warden of the petitioner's response, the decisions of the Warden and of the Assistant Deputy Commissioner "are quashed".

On March 1, 1993 the petitioner, through counsel, made such a response. On March 10 the Acting Warden notified the petitioner that a "staff review" led him to conclude that the transfer should be upheld. On April 16, 1993 the Assistant Deputy Commissioner issued a Notification of Transfer Decision. He approved the transfer to "Kent Institution, maximum security".

ISSUE OF RES JUDICATA

The respondents submitted that the whole matter is res judicata, having already been decided by Gow J. I did not uphold the objection in that Gow J. had not considered the "new" decisions. However, that did not mean that the decisions of Mr. Justice Gow were subject to review. That can be done only by an appeal.

Mr. Justice Gow declined "to quash the emergency transfer decision". He also held that "there has been a sharing to the greatest extent possible of the details of the alleged plan to escape." Consequently, the matters of the appropriateness of the emergency transfer and extent of the information provided are not open for review.

The petitioner further submitted that he has been prejudiced by the respondent's failure to charge him with a criminal offence or by the failure to proceed against him pursuant to the disciplinary sections of the Corrections and Conditional Release Act, S.C. 1992, c-20 and its regulations. Gow J. did not, in his reasons, discuss this issue. However, he found nothing wanting with the procedures other than the failure to provide adequate information in a timely manner. He carefully set out and reviewed the Directives that were followed. Implicit in his decisions is that the appropriate procedures, except as specifically stated, were followed up to the juncture of his judgments.

The same applies to the petitioner's submissions that his "sense of personal dignity" was breached contrary to s. 70 of the Corrections Act. It also applies to the contention that there was not compliance with the Commissioner's Directive 540.

I am of the opinion that Mr. Justice Gow's findings of fact and law, whether explicit or implicit, are binding upon me pursuant to the principles in Re Hansard Spruce Mills Limited [1954] 13 W.W.R. (N.S.) 285.

JUDICIAL REVIEW AND SECTION 7 OF THE CHARTER

The petitioner submitted as follows:

The petitioner says that his incarceration at Kent Institution is unlawful and that the decision to transfer him from William Head Institution was made in violation of the common law duty to act fairly and the principles of fundamental justice and was in violation of his constitutional rights pursuant to section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and in violation of the respondents' own rules of procedure and further, that in all of the circumstances, the decision was patently unreasonable.

The "patently unreasonable test" is commonly applied in applications asking for a judicial review. While the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.B.C. 1979, c. 209 was neither pleaded nor even mentioned on this hearing, both counsel treated the matter as such a review.

The petitioner submitted that in judicial reviews "an unreasonable error of fact is an error of law." He cited Blanchard v. Control Data Canada Ltd. [1984] 2 S.C.R. 476 (S.C.C.) which establishes the proposition that an unreasonable finding, whatever its origin, affects the jurisdiction of the tribunal.

The petitioner's submission contains the following:

Reasonableness in a carceral setting and, specifically, in relation to a decision of the National Parole Board was considered by Mr. Justice Walsh in Re Litwack and the National Parole Board. There, the court found that the National Parole Board's decision to refuse to vary their previous decision in the face of strong support by those writing reports about the parolee became so patently unfair as to require the intervention of the court. In setting aside the decision of the Board, Mr. Justice Walsh said:

"... It is not sufficient to comply merely with procedural fairness but also the substance of the decision must be reasonable on the facts." (Re Litwack and the National Parole Board (1986), 26 C.C.C. (3d) 65 at pp. 71 and 76 (F.C.T.D.)).

In Demaria v. Regional Transfer Board and Warden of Joyceville Institution, Reed, J., in a transfer case, expressed the view that both the Federal Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court of Canada have interpreted section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms as requiring not only that there be procedural fairness, in the narrow sense, but also that decisions not be made in an unreasonable or arbitrary manner. In her view, setting aside a decision of an administrative body on the ground that it is arbitrary or unreasonable as having been made without evidence to support it is one of the traditional grounds of judicial review and as such, within the concept of "fundamental justice". (Demaria v. Regional Transfer Board and Warden of Joyceville Institution (1898), 62 C.R. (3d) 248 at pp. 254-255 (F.C.T.D.)).

Counsel for the respondents paraphrased the words of Mr. Justice Seaton who, in a paper suggesting a definition for "patently unreasonable", said:

I do not think it can now be said that patently unreasonable errors are errors of a particular type as opposed to errors of a particular magnitude. I think that the test is rather, is this a decision that we can let stand?

Counsel for the respondents worded it this way: "Can the Court live with this decision, i.e. is it patently unreasonable?"

While counsel agreed to the application of the patently unreasonable test, they did not agree as to the standard to be applied. Counsel for the petitioner submitted that it should be "beyond a reasonable doubt or something very close thereto." He said that "just because it is administrative law, i.e. judicial review, does not change the underlying character of the matter which is criminal."

Counsel for the respondents contended that this hearing is by way of judicial review and that the standard to be applied is a balance of probabilities. I agree. The respondents carry the onus of establishing that the ultimate decision to transfer was, on a balance of probabilities, not patently unreasonable.

With respect to s. 7 of the Charter, it is well established that there is a requirement of fairness in the making of decisions to transfer inmates to higher security institutions. In Trono v. Gallant (1989) 68 C.R. (3d) 173 (Fed. C.A.) at page 180 Mr. Justice Pratte said:

Since the judgment of the Supreme Court of Canada in Martineau v. Matsqui Inst. Disciplinary Bd., supra, R. v. Miller, [1985] 2 S.C.R. 613, 49 C.R. (3d) 1, 16 Admin. L.R. 184, 3 C.C.C. (3d) 97, 24 D.L.R. (4th) 9, 14 O.A.C. 33, 63 N.R. 321, Cardinal v. Kent Inst., supra, and Morin v. Can. (Nat. Special Handling Unit Review Ctee.), [1985] 2 S.C.R. 662, 49 C.R. (3d) 26, 16 Admin. L.R. 264, 23 C.C.C. (3d) 132, 24 D.L.R. (4th) 71, 63 N.R. 363 [Que.], it can no longer be doubted that the decision to transfer an inmate to a penal institution where his freedom will be more severely restricted is, in effect, a committal to a "prison within a prison" which deprives the inmate of his liberty. Such a decision must therefore, according to s. 7 of the Charter, be made "in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice."

Section 7 of the Charter reads as follows:

Everyone has the right to life,liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice.

In Camphaug v. Canada (1990) 34 F.T.R. 165 (Fed. Ct.) Mr. Justice Strayer referred to the requirement for fairness in decisions transferring prisoners to higher security institutions. He said that:

This arises both under common law requirements of fairness and under s. 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms which requires that such decisions be taken in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice....The content of the fairness requirement will be the same whether it is ascribed to the common law or to the Charter. ... a decision to transfer is not like a conviction for an offence: what is required on the part of the decision-maker is a reasonable belief that the prisoner should be moved for the sake of the institution.

Consequently, the decision to transfer Mr. Fitzgerald must meet the tests of being fair pursuant to the principles enunciated under the Charter and of not being patently unreasonable in a judicial review.

THE EVIDENCE

The petitioner contended that the alleged escape as attributed to him by an informant is a fraud and a hoax. He suggested that if it came from one particular person it was designed to gain favour for that person within the corrections system. If it came from another person, it was because of animosity between that person and the petitioner. The petitioner generated a considerable amount of support for his position and has demonstrated that some people within the system feel that the transfer decision is unjust. I will, in a highly digested manner, review the affidavit evidence. Gary G. Fitzgerald - sworn November 18, 1992

Significant portions of this affidavit are reproduced in the reasons of Gow J. dated January 15, 1993. The petitioner details his proposal to renounce Canadian citizenship in favour of citizenship in another country. To this end he wrote to the consulates of several countries. His wife agreed to join him in a new country.

On October 19, 1992, the petitioner was sitting in the chapel when he was handcuffed and taken to the segregation unit. Then, pursuant to the Notification, he was transferred to Kent Institution. Mr. Fitzgerald alleges that he knows the identity of the informant "who had reasons to falsely implicate me in an escape plan."

The petitioner notes that he was scheduled to see the Transfer Board from Ferndale Institution, a minimum security facility, on October 23 and that he had the "full support of the Case Management Team at William Head Institution." The Progress Summary Report states:

It is the feeling of the C.M.T. that it would be in the best interest of Mr. Fitzgerald that he be afforded this transfer. His wife is prepared to move, and he has further family support on the mainland. He has completed all of the programmes that we have to offer at W.H.I. Rheta Fitzgerald - sworn November 23, 1993

The petitioner's wife attests to the truth of those portions of her husband's affidavit concerning moving to another country. Marvin Goertz - sworn November 23, 1992

Mr. Goertz is the Chaplain of William Head Institution. He acknowledges that he discussed the citizenship issues with Mr. Fitzgerald. Gilles Pinard - sworn November 30, 1992

Mr. Pinard had discussed with the petitioner the concept of renouncing Canadian citizenship and moving to another country. Christopher Wilson - sworn November 26, 1992

Mr. Wilson is a former inmate of William Head Institution. He had discussions with Mr. Fitzgerald about citizenship and residency. Gary G. Fitzgerald - sworn December 11, 1992

Portions of this affidavit are reproduced in the reasons of Gow J. dated January 15, 1993. The petitioner deposes that he believes the informant to be Michael Rennie, co-inmate at William Head Institution. Mr. Fitzgerald believes that Mr. Rennie was "induced by threats or promises of reward from another inmate to fabricate allegations" of an escape plan. The petitioner says that Mr. Rennie is unreliable as a source and that he is "dishonest and deceitful." Arthur M. Trono - sworn December 29, 1992

Mr. Trono is the Warden of William Head Institution. Portions of this affidavit are contained in Mr. Justice Gow's reasons of January 15, 1993.

At this juncture the hearing took place before Mr. Justice Gow. It was heard on January 6, 1993 and his reasons followed on January 15. In response to the reasons Mr. Trono authored a further affidavit sworn on January 20, 1993. Most of this affidavit is reproduced in Gow J.'s reasons of February 25, 1993. Jason Duder - sworn January 25, 1993

Mr. Duder is a stepson of the petitioner. He read Mr. Trono's affidavit of January 20 and in response denies any involvement in an escape plan. He denies having access to a boat and says that he would never assist the petitioner to escape. He further denies any conversation with the petitioner on the subject of escape. Sandra Walbaum - sworn January 25, 1993

Mr. Walbaum denies having "ever heard of the petitioner Gary G. Fitzgerald." She adds that she would not assist him to escape. Chaplain Marvin Goertz - sworn February 2, 1993

Chaplain Goertz read Mr. Trono's affidavit. With reference to the petitioner's affidavit, Rev. Goertz says that it is true that the petitioner's letters to consulates were kept in the chapel and that he talked to him about the "purchase of a sales package from a helicopter company."

He further states that he believes that the National Parole Board granted Mr. Rennie "a parole release to the maritimes." Gary G. Fitzgerald - sworn January 29, 1993

He refers to the affidavit of Mr. Trono and denies any escape plan. He deals specifically with the details of the plan as revealed by Mr. Trono. Mr. Fitzgerald concludes as follows:

THAT I believe that it is normal and in accordance with fundamental principles of justice in Canada that a person accused of this type of an offence would be entitled to know the full case against him and have an opportunity to confront his accusers by cross-examination and to otherwise be given a fair opportunity to respond including an opportunity to show the lack of credibility of the informants and consequently that the procedure that is being followed here is grossly unfair and in violation of principles of fundamental justice in that I have not been charged with any offence nor have I been given an opportunity to have a fair trial on the allegations and consequently this process is circumventing the principles of fundamental justice and has deprived me of my relative liberty.

A further hearing was held before Gow J. on February 5, 1993 and his reasons were delivered on February 25. On March 1, 1993 Mr. V. Barrett, counsel for the petitioner, wrote to Mr. Trono. He reiterated some of the petitioner's responses to the suggested escape plan. He also supplied further information. Mr. Fitzgerald authored two detailed narratives in support of his "appeal to Mr. Arthur Trono." On March 29, 1993 counsel for the petitioner wrote a lengthy letter to Mr. J. Duggan, Deputy Commissioner, Correctional Service of Canada. He pleaded Mr. Fitzgerald's case and noted that Gow J.'s decision would be appealed to the British Columbia Court of Appeal.

On March 2 and 3 Mr. Barrett and Mr. Conroy made oral representations to Mr. Trono, Mr. Denis (Deputy Warden) and Mr. R. Scott (Assistant Warden). On April 16, 1993 the Acting Deputy Commissioner approved the petitioner's transfer to Kent Institution and gave these reasons:

Mr. Fitzgerald, age 30, is serving a life sentence for Murder and Contempt of Court, commencing in 1981. Mr. Fitzgerald was transferred on an emergency involuntary basis, to Kent Institution, based on believed reliable detailed inmate informant information indicating that Mr. Fitzgerald was planning to escape from William Head Institution with other inmates. A brief summary of this information is as follows; The escape plan was reported to have involved Mr. Fitzgerald acquiring false identification, being picked up by boat from William Head Institution, travel to Vancouver, and air travel to, eventually, Costa Rica. This informant information was reportedly supported by the discovery of correspondence from Mr. Fitzgerald to the consulates of various countries, including Costa Rica, inquiring about residency criteria. The inmate informant was not identified out of consideration for the individual's safety. The other inmates involved were not identified as it was believed that this information would cause the informant to be identified.

The above information was provided to Mr. Fitzgerald in the 1993-02-25 Judgment of Mr. Justice Gow. Mr. Justice Gow directed that Mr. Fitzgerald be provided an opportunity to respond to the supplementary affidavit, dated 1993-01-20, information presented to the court by Mr. A. Trono, Warden, William Head Institution. In the Notification of Review and Recommendation Relative To Transfer dated 1993-03-10 it is clearly stated that Mr. Fitzgerald's written responses as well as a 1993-03-01 submission and attachments from his legal counsel. Mr. Vaughan Barrett, were considered in the recommendation that Mr. Fitzgerald be transferred involuntarily to Kent Institution. This same information was considered in this decision. A further submission dated 1993-03-29, submitted on Mr. Fitzgerald's behalf, by Mr. Barrett, was also received and considered.

A summary of the information submitted on Mr. Fitzgerald's behalf is as follows: Mr. Fitzgerald denies any intent or plan to escape from William Head Institution; he references his positive behaviour over the last 8 years; treatment at the Regional Psychiatric Centre (Pac); institutional support for transfer to minimum security; and, family support. Mr. Fitzgerald's submissions, in addition to those of others, provide his explanation of letters to foreign consulates. Those individuals identified in the community who were reported to be of assistance to Mr. Fitzgerald in the escape plan have submitted statements denying knowledge of such a plan and willingness to participate. Mr. Fitzgerald, in his own submissions, and in those prepared on his behalf, has addressed, in detail, the information presented in the Warden's supplementary affidavit. He has also questioned why he as not considered for transfer to Mission Institution rather than to maximum security. Transfer to maximum security is acknowledged to have significant negative consequences for Mr. Fitzgerald.

The Warden's decision and rationale for upholding the recommendation that Mr. Fitzgerald be transferred to Kent Institution are noted. While submissions presented by both Mr. Fitzgerald and his legal counsel do provide alternate explanations for much of the information and present the theory that the scape plan was a hoax, the informant information is considered credible, primarily due to the level of detail provided. Mr. Fitzgerald's escape attempt in 1984, and the discovered correspondence to foreign consulates does support the belief that Mr. Fitzgerald was planning to escape. I am not convinced that the escape plot was a fabrication by the informant. Information that would indicate the informant's motivation to provide false information has not been presented. I am also concerned about the level of risk Mr. Fitzgerald would present to the community should he escape.

On April 26, 1993, the petitioner filed a "grievance". It was denied on August 18, 1993. This petition was launched on August 12, 1993 supported by the following affidavits: Gary F. Fitzgerald - sworn August 11, 1993

He traces the history of the legal proceedings. He also names Mr. Patrick Kelly as the informant. In a supplementary affidavit sworn on September 23, 1993 he speaks more about Mr. Kelly. Jack Nelson - sworn November 16, 1993

Mr. Nelson is an Internal Preventive Security Officer at Ferndale Institution. His affidavit is sworn "in support" of the petitioner. David Hamer - sworn November 22, 1993

Mr. Hamer has been a Corrections Officer for 23 years. He responds to Mr. Trono's affidavit of January 20, 1993. He details his seizure of the petitioner's documents from the chapel. He deposes regarding Mr. Kelly. He concluded that "there was absolutely no truth to the allegation" of an escape plan. This conclusion appears to be based upon the assumption that Mr. Kelly is the informant. Chaplain Marvin Goertz - sworn November 26, 1993

Chaplain Goertz speaks of matters deposed to in earlier affidavits and also he speaks about Mr. Kelly. Vaughan Barrett - sworn December 13, 1993

Mr. Barrett details his dealings with the petitioner. Gary G. Fitzgerald - sworn April 12, 1994

The petitioner details the grievance procedure and suggests a misinterpretation of the judgment of Gow J. He speaks about Mr. Kelly and annexes a letter from the Corrections Service that states that Mr. Kelly is not the informant. Mr. Fitzgerald also said that Mr. Rennie had contacted Mr. Conroy and had denied being the informer.

JUDGMENT

Mr. Conroy submitted that the decision to transfer was arbitrary, unreasonable, a breach of fundamental justice and thereby unsupportable. In considering these submissions I must keep in mind that the decision to transfer was made by authorities who know infinitely more about the incarceration and corrections systems than does the Court. The authorities should be secure to think that if they act in accordance with fundamental principles of justice within the legislative requirements, that their decisions will prevail.

In Plamondon v. W.C.B. (B.C.) et al. (1988) 21 B.C.L.R. (2d) 261 (S.C.B.C.), Mr. Justice Shaw noted that there is a "delicate balance to be maintained" in court reviews of the decisions of tribunals. He said that the courts must leave a broad area within which statutory tribunals may operate and that this is why "mere errors of law and fact are not reviewable. He continued:

It is desirable that the decisions of statutory tribunals, wherever possible, be prompt and final. These tribunals often exercise jurisdiction which involves specialized knowledge and skills that the courts do not have.

The courts should stand poised to intervene only in those cases wherein either breaches of fundamental justice have occurred or where there is such unreasonableness to a decision that it amounts to unfairness. The error should be egregious or equivalent to bad faith: Syndicat des Employee de Production du Quebec v. C.L.R.B. [1984] 2 S.C.R. 412. Even if the authorities are acting in an administrative capacity, rather than a quasi-judicial one, they must act honestly and fairly. This was commented upon by Roskill L.J. in Re Liverpool Taxi Owners' Association [1972] 2 All E.R. 589 at 596: The power of the court to intervene is not limited, as once was thought, to those cases where the function in question is judicial or quasi-judicial. The modern cases show that this court will intervene more widely than in the past. Even where the function is said to be administrative, the court will not hesitate to intervene in a suitable case if it is necessary in order to secure fairness.

In Martineau v. Matsqui Institution Disciplinary Board [1980] 1 S.C.R. 602 Dickson J. said at page 619:

When concerned with individual cases and aggrieved persons, there is the tendency to forget that one is dealing with public law remedies, which, when granted by the courts, not only set aright individual injustice, but also ensure that public bodies exercising powers affecting citizens heed the jurisdiction granted them. ... To give a narrow or technical interpretation to "rights" in an individual sense is to misconceive the broader purpose of judicial review of administrative action. One should, I suggest, begin with the premise that any public body exercising power over subjects may be amenable to judicial supervision, the individual interest involved being but one factor to be considered in resolving the broad policy question of the nature of review appropriate for the particular administrative body.

Within the confines of the "delicate balancing act" I have concerns about the decision to transfer. One is the conclusion in the Assistant Deputy Commissioner's reasons that he was "concerned about the level of risk Mr. Fitzgerald would present to the community should he escape." The Case Management Team supported Mr. Fitzgerald's transfer to Ferndale Institution, a minimum security facility. It said as follows:

Mr. Fitzgerald has successfully cascaded through the judicial system and he has taken appropriate programmes to correct and modify aberrant behaviour patterns that lead to his criminal actions, and he has matured with the passage of time. He feels that the time he spent attending programmes at the Regional Psychiatric Centre led him to a clearer picture of his inadequacies and the steps necessary to overcome them.

This makes it difficult to understand the basis for the conclusion of the Assistant Deputy Commissioner which suggests some significant level of risk to the public from Mr. Fitzgerald. There is nothing in the Reasons of Acting Warden D.J. Denis about this. He simply upheld the transfer on the basis that the "escape plot was very real."

Another of my concerns is the finding that the "escape plot was very real." The plan is so inept as to put its existence into doubt. The petitioner was to be picked up in a small boat off the shore of William Head Institution. The likelihood of this event being observed seems significant. Mr. Fitzgerald was then to make his way to Nanaimo and then, by way of B.C. Ferries, to Vancouver. He would then, accompanied by his wife, depart on an international flight with the ultimate destination being Costa Rica.

Mr. Denis found that the "explanation in relation to correspondence with various countries as to residency is simply unbelievable, at best naive." There is, in my opinion, a vast difference in the context of this case between "unbelievable" and "naive". If it is unbelievable then Mr. Denis would be justified in concluding that it was part of an escape plot. However, if it was simply naive then it can have no bearing on the authenticity of the alleged plot.

The Assistant Deputy Commissioner said that the information from the informant, an inmate, "is considered credible, primarily due to the level of detail provided. Mr. Fitzgerald's escape attempt in 1984, and the discovered correspondence to foreign consulates does support the belief that Mr. Fitzgerald was planning to escape." The Assistant Deputy Commissioner then concluded that he is "not convinced that the escape plot was a fabrication by the informant. Information that would indicate the informant's motivation to provide false information has not been presented."

It would be more convincing if he had concluded that the plot was a fabrication. Furthermore, in that the authorities would not provide to the petitioner the name of the informant, it was hardly open to the petitioner to provide "information that would indicate the informant's motivation to provide false information."

There is also the problem created by the fact that much "material" is contained in a sealed envelope. I am not suggesting that the contents should be revealed to the petitioner. In any event, Mr. Justice Gow held that information has been shared with the petitioner to the greatest extent possible. However, that does not change the situation created by the concealment of information. There can be no doubt but that the petitioner is in a disabled position to respond fully to the concerns of the authorities.

This, in my opinion, puts more of an onus on the authorities to ensure that the procedures followed are in keeping with the regulations and the procedures set forth. Further, that the reasons given for decisions are sound and as revealing as possible as to the foundations therefore. In this case I am unable to conclude that the reasons properly reflect the evidence, including what is contained in the sealed envelope.

While the authorities chose to find the informant reliable, and this is a subjective judgment, I cannot uncover any basis for this. Nothing in the informant's background suggests to me that he is a person to be relied upon.

The climate today is against a "soft" attitude towards prisoner's rights. This is understandable in view of the crimes apparently committed by persons on parole or who have escaped from correction institutions. However, I cannot allow this atmosphere to govern the outcome of this petition.

It appears to me, in keeping with the submissions of counsel for the petitioner, that the decisions to transfer have been extremely arbitrary and, as such, unfair to the petitioner. The reasons and decisions do not conform to the principle of natural justice which, after all, is only "fair play in action": Ridge v. Baldwin [1963] 1 Q.B. 539, [1962] 1 All E.R. 834.

The decision to transfer is not, to again return to the words of Mr. Justice Seaton, "a decision that we can let stand." I am of the opinion that it has not been demonstrated that the decision was arrived at fairly. It is therefore "patently unreasonable".

REMEDY

Counsel for the petitioner said that this court has broad discretion as to the form of order. Counsel for the respondents submitted that it is only open to order that the petitioner be returned to William Head Institution or that the application be dismissed. He conceded that if there is a breach of fundamental justice or if the decision is patently unreasonable, the petitioner deserves a remedy.

Relying upon Gough v. Canada (National Parole Board), [1991] C.F. 171 (Fed. C.A.), counsel for the petitioner submitted that Mr. Justice Gow erred in not "finding a remedy for Mr. Fitzgerald." In Gough, Mahoney J.A., with whom the other judges concurred, said at page 175:

I sympathize with the learned trial judge not wishing to take the responsibility for putting a parolee back on the street when she knows that there may be a good, but undivulged, reason by that would be unwise. As she said, "To quash an order of the Parole Board, in a situation such as the present, is not something which should be lightly undertaken." That, however, misconceives the Court's responsibility. It is the responsibility of the Parole Board both to take the parolee off the street and to justify that decision in the context of the parolee's constitutional rights. If it fails to justify the decision, when challenged, it is the Court's responsibility to give effect to the parolee's constitutional rights.

In my respectful opinion, the Board is correct. An order requiring the party that has been found to have infringed the constitutional rights of another to produce evidence that may establish that the infringement was justified, cannot, by any reasonable stretch of language or imagination, be held to be a remedy, appropriate and just in the circumstances, granted to the aggrieved party. [my underlining].

It is not for me to say what Mr. Justice Gow should have done. However, I agree with the submissions as supported by the passages quoted from Gough. To send the matter back to the respondents for yet another decision would not afford Mr. Fitzgerald any remedy for the breaches that I have found to prevail. As with the trial judge in Gough, to quash an order of the Assistant Deputy Commissioner "is not something that should be lightly undertaken." However, that decision has been challenged and, in my opinion, successfully.

The only appropriate remedy available is to return Mr. Fitzgerald to William Head Institution.

THACKRAY J. DRS/DRS
